<insights>

- High coupling (0.6) — 2 imports target "web-viewer"
- Moderate cohesion (0.63) and coupling (0.38) are acceptable for a small service cluster, but the zone only has 4 files — merging it back into the main web zone would simplify navigation
- Bidirectional imports with dashboard-mcp-server (2 each way) create a coupling loop that could be resolved by having the scheduler depend on the tracker unidirectionally
- Both source files have corresponding unit tests co-located in the same zone, which is good for discoverability
- Bidirectional imports between this zone and dashboard-mcp-server (2 imports in each direction) create a coupling cycle; consider inverting the dependency or introducing an event/callback boundary.
- At only 4 files, this zone is a candidate for consolidation into the parent web zone to reduce fragmentation.
- The cleanup scheduler paired with an incremental recorder is a sound separation of concerns for analytics data management.
- Is the only zone in the web package that forms a cycle with another zone, making it the sole structural violation of the DAG property in the inter-zone graph
- The bidirectional coupling distinguishes it from websocket-realtime-layer, which accepts inbound imports from dashboard-mcp-server unidirectionally — two different coupling patterns exist among the functional satellites
- task-usage-tracking is the only zone creating a cycle in the web package inter-zone graph (bidirectional with dashboard-mcp-server). All other inter-zone edges are unidirectional. Resolving this cycle would make the full web zone graph a DAG.
- The bidirectional cycle with dashboard-mcp-server is most likely caused by the cleanup scheduler requiring application-level infrastructure (timer registration, shutdown hooks) owned by the hub — dependency injection at construction time (passing a timer interface into the scheduler) would sever the back-edge without extracting a new module
- The two source files have asymmetric roles: incremental-task-usage.ts is a pure recorder (data-in) while usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts is an orchestrator (data-out trigger) — these asymmetric roles suggest different ownership and reinforce that the scheduler's back-import into dashboard-mcp-server is the specific edge to cut
- usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts imports from dashboard-mcp-server to access infrastructure it should receive via constructor injection — inverting this dependency is the minimal targeted fix for the only cycle in the web package inter-zone graph
- This zone is the only zone in the project that simultaneously fails both cohesion (0.63 < 0.7 threshold) AND has non-trivial coupling (0.38) — it meets the dual fragility criterion described in the severity guide, making it the highest-risk small zone in the codebase
- The name incremental-task-usage.ts is ambiguous — 'incremental' is a vague qualifier that does not communicate the recorder/write role of the file; the paired usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts has a more descriptive name, creating an asymmetric naming convention within the same zone
- task-usage-tracking is the only zone with BOTH cohesion below 0.7 (0.63) AND coupling above 0.3 (0.38) simultaneously — this dual fragility makes it the highest structural risk among small zones. Resolve the bidirectional cycle with dashboard-mcp-server via constructor injection as previously identified, then re-evaluate whether cohesion improves once the back-edge is removed.
- Rename incremental-task-usage.ts to task-usage-recorder.ts to create a symmetric naming pair with usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts. Both files are now named by their role (recorder, scheduler) rather than by an implementation detail ('incremental'), which improves navigability and onboarding clarity.
- [call graph] 100 internal calls, 1 outgoing, 1 incoming (cohesion: 0.99, coupling: 0.01)

</insights>
